sources.notes: Hard version asks to output a satisfactory tree if it exists.
Official editorial partitions by suffix maxima and connects each block internally and to a previous prefix minimum.

annotation.summary: Given a permutation, either prove no satisfactory tree exists or explicitly output one.
annotation.constraints: A linear construction is needed over the permutation.
annotation.core_idea: First use the same prefix-minimum/suffix-maximum cut test as the easy version. If feasible, split the permutation into blocks ending at suffix maximum positions; within each block connect all earlier values to that block's suffix maximum, then connect each block to a previous prefix minimum to make all blocks connected.

solution_variants.name: suffix-max-block-tree
solution_variants.summary: Use suffix maxima as block roots and prefix minima as inter-block connectors.

tags.evidence: The hard version requires explicitly constructing the tree edges.
Both the feasibility proof and construction use prefix minima and suffix maxima.
The output structure is a tree built by connecting vertices according to the permutation-derived graph condition.
